relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Moab receives a covenant renewal beyond Horeb.
Moses calls the people back to what their own eyes witnessed in Egypt.
Trials, signs, and wonders remain within communal memory.
Seeing events is not the same as receiving a heart that understands them.
Forty years are remembered through clothing and sandals that endured.
Wilderness dependence became a lesson in knowing YHWH.
The speech again remembers victory over Sihon and Og.
Their land becomes inheritance east of Jordan.
Covenant wisdom is expected to become embodied action.
The whole people stands before YHWH.
Children, women, immigrants, woodcutters, and water drawers stand inside the same covenant assembly.
No social rank is allowed to stand outside the oath.
The covenant establishes mutual belonging between YHWH and the people.
The oath is not cut with the visible generation alone.
Those absent and not yet born are drawn into the future of the words.
Egypt and the surrounding nations remain remembered contexts.
The people have seen manufactured gods of wood, stone, silver, and gold.
Hidden turning of heart is pictured as a root producing poison.
Private reassurance cannot cancel public covenant responsibility.
The oath language reaches toward erasure of the defiant name.
The individual is separated beneath the written covenant sanctions.
Future children and distant foreigners inspect the wounded land.
Salt, sulfur, and barrenness turn landscape into testimony.
The nations ask what could produce such devastation.
The inherited explanation is abandonment of the covenant of liberation.
Other allegiances replaced the One who brought them out.
The land bears the curse rhetoric of the scroll.
Uprooting and exile become the covenant's catastrophic reversal.
Hidden things remain with YHWH; what is revealed is given for faithful practice.

contemplative_note: Deuteronomy 29 places everyone inside the covenant—leaders, children, women, immigrants, and laborers—and then places a boundary around certainty. Hidden things belong to YHWH. Human responsibility begins with what is revealed, not with fantasies about secret enemies or concealed cosmic knowledge.
